name: 1919 Black Sox
description: The 1919 Black Sox scandal refers to allegations that members of the Chicago White Sox intentionally lost the 1919 World Series against the Cincinnati Reds in exchange for money from gamblers. The conspiracy theory posits that eight White Sox players, including stars like Shoeless Joe Jackson and Eddie Cicotte, were bribed to throw the series. The motivation is believed to have stemmed from dissatisfaction with White Sox owner Charles Comiskey's perceived stinginess and unfair treatment of players. Proponents point to statistical anomalies in the players' performances during the series, confessions (later recanted) by some players, and circumstantial evidence of meetings with gamblers as proof. The mainstream perspective acknowledges the scandal as a historical event, resulting in the lifetime banishment of the eight players involved. However, the extent of each player's involvement and the degree to which the series was truly 'thrown' remains debated by historians. Some argue that the players' performances were impacted by pressure and intimidation, rather than outright intentional sabotage. Others believe that while a conspiracy existed, the impact on the outcome of the games may have been overstated. The scandal had a lasting impact on baseball, leading to the creation of the Commissioner of Baseball position to restore public trust in the sport.
subtitle: Baseball's infamous throwing scandal.

claims.claim: Eight White Sox players intentionally threw the 1919 World Series.
claims.evidence: Confessions of players (later recanted), statistical anomalies, meetings with gamblers.
claims.criticism: Confessions were obtained under duress and are unreliable. Statistical anomalies can be attributed to other factors. Not all players involved acted similarly.

claims.claim: Charles Comiskey's stinginess motivated the players to accept bribes.
claims.evidence: Comiskey's reputation for being cheap, low salaries compared to other teams.
claims.criticism: While Comiskey was known for frugality, it doesn't definitively prove a direct link to the players' actions. Other factors like gambling debts could have played a role.

claims.claim: The outcome of the series was predetermined by the fix.
claims.evidence: Gambler involvement, alleged signals between players and gamblers.
claims.criticism: The Reds were a strong team and may have won regardless. The extent of the fix's impact on the games is debated.

keyFigures.name: Arnold Rothstein
keyFigures.role: Alleged mastermind gambler behind the fix.

keyFigures.name: Shoeless Joe Jackson
keyFigures.role: Star player accused of involvement in the fix.

keyFigures.name: Eddie Cicotte
keyFigures.role: Pitcher who confessed to throwing games.

keyFigures.name: Charles Comiskey
keyFigures.role: Owner of the Chicago White Sox, whose frugality is seen as a contributing factor.

keyFigures.name: Eliot Asinof
keyFigures.role: Author of 'Eight Men Out', a definitive book on the scandal

timeline.event: Chicago White Sox lose the World Series to the Cincinnati Reds.
Grand jury investigates allegations of a fix.
Trial of the eight players ends in acquittal, but they are banned from baseball by Commissioner Landis.
Debate continues regarding the extent of each player's involvement and the impact of the fix.

sources.author: Steven A. Riess
sources.work: Touching Base: Professional Baseball and American Culture in the Progressive Era
sources.title: Steven A. Riess
